  • Greater awareness of auction processes among mainstream sellers
  • Professional investors actively seeking stock at all price points

Considering property auctions?

For those contemplating buying or selling at auction, it could be worth understanding the key differences from conventional property transactions. Auction purchases typically require:

  • Immediate exchange of contracts with a binding commitment
  • Payment of a non-refundable deposit on the fall of the hammer
  • Completion usually within 28 days

Prospective buyers might wish to thoroughly research properties beforehand, as auction purchases don't allow for the same due diligence periods as standard sales. Obtaining legal advice and arranging finance in advance remains crucial, as mortgage applications need to be completed within tight timeframes.

The recent growth in auction activity reflects the market's adaptability, offering alternative routes for property transactions that suit different circumstances. As always, seeking professional advice before committing to any property purchase method remains essential.

item.title: Home building registrations fall in Q1 as rising costs threaten Q2
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market has seen a notable slowdown in new home construction during the first quarter of 2026, with industry data showing a decline in building registrations. Market analysts suggest this reflects ongoing challenges including construction cost inflation, affordability constraints and subdued buyer demand.

Construction sector faces mounting pressures

Housebuilders appear to be scaling back development plans amid multiple economic headwinds. Industry reports indicate several factors contributing to the slowdown:

  • Rising material and labour costs squeezing profit margins
  • Higher mortgage rates reducing buyer affordability
  • Weaker demand in some regional markets
  • Ongoing planning system delays

The combination of these pressures has led some developers to reassess project viability, with particular caution around schemes targeting first-time buyers or lower price brackets where affordability pressures bite hardest.

Outlook remains uncertain for coming quarters

Market observers suggest the second quarter of 2026 may see further constraints unless conditions improve. Key concerns for the sector include:

  • Potential for further interest rate rises maintaining mortgage affordability pressures
  • Construction cost inflation showing limited signs of easing
  • Some developers reportedly delaying starts on permitted sites

However, the picture varies significantly by region, with some areas continuing to see robust demand. Industry bodies have called for targeted support to maintain housing delivery, particularly for affordable and energy-efficient homes.

For prospective buyers, the slowdown in new builds may mean fewer options in some markets, potentially maintaining upward pressure on prices for available properties. Those considering new build purchases might wish to research local market conditions thoroughly and explore all available mortgage options.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market has seen a notable slowdown in new home construction during the first quarter of 2026, with industry data showing a decline in building registrations. Market analysts suggest this reflects ongoing challenges including construction cost inflation, affordability constraints and subdued buyer demand.

Construction sector faces mounting pressures

Housebuilders appear to be scaling back development plans amid multiple economic headwinds. Industry reports indicate several factors contributing to the slowdown:

  • Rising material and labour costs squeezing profit margins
  • Higher mortgage rates reducing buyer affordability
  • Weaker demand in some regional markets
  • Ongoing planning system delays

The combination of these pressures has led some developers to reassess project viability, with particular caution around schemes targeting first-time buyers or lower price brackets where affordability pressures bite hardest.

Outlook remains uncertain for coming quarters

Market observers suggest the second quarter of 2026 may see further constraints unless conditions improve. Key concerns for the sector include:

  • Potential for further interest rate rises maintaining mortgage affordability pressures
  • Construction cost inflation showing limited signs of easing
  • Some developers reportedly delaying starts on permitted sites

However, the picture varies significantly by region, with some areas continuing to see robust demand. Industry bodies have called for targeted support to maintain housing delivery, particularly for affordable and energy-efficient homes.

For prospective buyers, the slowdown in new builds may mean fewer options in some markets, potentially maintaining upward pressure on prices for available properties. Those considering new build purchases might wish to research local market conditions thoroughly and explore all available mortgage options.

item.title: What you need to know when buying investment properties in Portugal
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Portugal has become an increasingly popular destination for property investors, thanks to its attractive climate, thriving tourism industry, and potential for high rental yields. However, purchasing an investment property in this southern European country comes with its own set of considerations, from tax implications to visa requirements. For those exploring this avenue, understanding the intricacies of the Portuguese property market is essential.

Navigating Tax Requirements

One of the first aspects to consider when buying property in Portugal is the tax landscape. Investors may be subject to several taxes, including stamp duty, property transfer tax, and annual municipal taxes. Stamp duty typically applies at a rate of 0.8% of the property’s purchase price, whilst property transfer tax (IMT) varies depending on the value and location of the property. Additionally, homeowners must pay an annual municipal tax (IMI), which is calculated based on the property’s rateable value.

For those looking to generate rental income, it’s worth noting that Portugal operates a progressive income tax system on rental earnings, with rates ranging from 14.5% to 48%. Non-residents may also be subject to withholding tax on rental income. Seeking advice from a local tax expert could help ensure compliance and optimise tax efficiency.

Understanding Visa Options

Portugal offers several visa programmes that may appeal to property investors. The Golden Visa scheme, in particular, has gained significant attention. This programme grants residency to non-EU citizens who invest in Portuguese real estate, provided the investment meets a minimum threshold, typically €500,000. However, recent changes to the scheme mean that properties in high-density areas like Lisbon and Porto may no longer qualify, prompting investors to explore opportunities in other regions.

For those planning to spend extended periods in Portugal, the D7 visa is another option. This visa is designed for individuals with sufficient financial means to support themselves, including income from rental properties. It’s advisable to consult with an immigration specialist to determine the most suitable visa route based on individual circumstances.

Identifying High-Yield Opportunities

Portugal’s property market offers diverse investment opportunities, particularly in cities like Lisbon, Porto, and the Algarve region. These areas are known for their strong rental demand, driven by tourism and a growing expat community. Coastal properties, in particular, often yield higher returns due to their appeal to holidaymakers.

Beyond traditional buy-to-let investments, some investors may consider renovating older properties to add value. Portugal’s rich architectural heritage includes many properties ripe for refurbishment, particularly in rural areas. However, it’s important to factor in renovation costs and local planning regulations when assessing potential returns.

Investing in Portuguese property can be a rewarding venture, but it requires careful planning and research. From understanding tax obligations to exploring visa options and identifying lucrative opportunities, thorough preparation is key. Engaging local experts, such as tax advisors and real estate agents, could help investors navigate the process more effectively and maximise their returns.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Portugal has become an increasingly popular destination for property investors, thanks to its attractive climate, thriving tourism industry, and potential for high rental yields. However, purchasing an investment property in this southern European country comes with its own set of considerations, from tax implications to visa requirements. For those exploring this avenue, understanding the intricacies of the Portuguese property market is essential.

Navigating Tax Requirements

One of the first aspects to consider when buying property in Portugal is the tax landscape. Investors may be subject to several taxes, including stamp duty, property transfer tax, and annual municipal taxes. Stamp duty typically applies at a rate of 0.8% of the property’s purchase price, whilst property transfer tax (IMT) varies depending on the value and location of the property. Additionally, homeowners must pay an annual municipal tax (IMI), which is calculated based on the property’s rateable value.

For those looking to generate rental income, it’s worth noting that Portugal operates a progressive income tax system on rental earnings, with rates ranging from 14.5% to 48%. Non-residents may also be subject to withholding tax on rental income. Seeking advice from a local tax expert could help ensure compliance and optimise tax efficiency.

Understanding Visa Options

Portugal offers several visa programmes that may appeal to property investors. The Golden Visa scheme, in particular, has gained significant attention. This programme grants residency to non-EU citizens who invest in Portuguese real estate, provided the investment meets a minimum threshold, typically €500,000. However, recent changes to the scheme mean that properties in high-density areas like Lisbon and Porto may no longer qualify, prompting investors to explore opportunities in other regions.

For those planning to spend extended periods in Portugal, the D7 visa is another option. This visa is designed for individuals with sufficient financial means to support themselves, including income from rental properties. It’s advisable to consult with an immigration specialist to determine the most suitable visa route based on individual circumstances.

Identifying High-Yield Opportunities

Portugal’s property market offers diverse investment opportunities, particularly in cities like Lisbon, Porto, and the Algarve region. These areas are known for their strong rental demand, driven by tourism and a growing expat community. Coastal properties, in particular, often yield higher returns due to their appeal to holidaymakers.

Beyond traditional buy-to-let investments, some investors may consider renovating older properties to add value. Portugal’s rich architectural heritage includes many properties ripe for refurbishment, particularly in rural areas. However, it’s important to factor in renovation costs and local planning regulations when assessing potential returns.

Investing in Portuguese property can be a rewarding venture, but it requires careful planning and research. From understanding tax obligations to exploring visa options and identifying lucrative opportunities, thorough preparation is key. Engaging local experts, such as tax advisors and real estate agents, could help investors navigate the process more effectively and maximise their returns.

item.title: Property firm expands with launch of new estate agency
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Yorkshire-based property specialists Mr & Mr Child have expanded their operations with the launch of a new estate agency, Child & Co Estates. The move marks a strategic growth for the founders, James and Steven Child, who established their original boutique agency in May 2024 with a focus on distinctive, high-value properties.

Expanding services across Yorkshire

The new venture broadens the company's offerings to include residential sales, lettings and property management services for a wider range of Yorkshire properties. Whilst Mr & Mr Child will maintain its niche focus on unique homes with an average listing price of £900,000, Child & Co Estates aims to deliver the same high standards of service to a more diverse clientele.

The expansion follows growing demand from homeowners and landlords impressed by the founders' bespoke approach. Over the past two years, the original agency has grown from a two-person operation to a five-strong team, with about 24% of its properties sold off-market through targeted buyer matching strategies.

A thoughtful approach to growth

James Child explained: "We deliberately kept our initial focus narrow, concentrating on delivering exceptional service for distinctive properties. The positive response has shown there's appetite for our standards across different market segments."

Steven Child emphasised their commitment to maintaining quality during expansion: "This isn't about becoming a high-volume agency. We're building on our foundations with the same values - professional standards combined with personal service."

The new agency will be supported by the existing Mr & Mr Child team, with both businesses operating under the founders' oversight. The expansion reflects Yorkshire's evolving property landscape, where homeowners increasingly value tailored services alongside traditional estate agency offerings.

For those considering property services in Yorkshire, the launch provides another option combining boutique-style attention with broader market expertise. As the property market continues to shift, such strategic expansions may become more common amongst successful niche operators.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Yorkshire-based property specialists Mr & Mr Child have expanded their operations with the launch of a new estate agency, Child & Co Estates. The move marks a strategic growth for the founders, James and Steven Child, who established their original boutique agency in May 2024 with a focus on distinctive, high-value properties.

Expanding services across Yorkshire

The new venture broadens the company's offerings to include residential sales, lettings and property management services for a wider range of Yorkshire properties. Whilst Mr & Mr Child will maintain its niche focus on unique homes with an average listing price of £900,000, Child & Co Estates aims to deliver the same high standards of service to a more diverse clientele.

The expansion follows growing demand from homeowners and landlords impressed by the founders' bespoke approach. Over the past two years, the original agency has grown from a two-person operation to a five-strong team, with about 24% of its properties sold off-market through targeted buyer matching strategies.

A thoughtful approach to growth

James Child explained: "We deliberately kept our initial focus narrow, concentrating on delivering exceptional service for distinctive properties. The positive response has shown there's appetite for our standards across different market segments."

Steven Child emphasised their commitment to maintaining quality during expansion: "This isn't about becoming a high-volume agency. We're building on our foundations with the same values - professional standards combined with personal service."

The new agency will be supported by the existing Mr & Mr Child team, with both businesses operating under the founders' oversight. The expansion reflects Yorkshire's evolving property landscape, where homeowners increasingly value tailored services alongside traditional estate agency offerings.

For those considering property services in Yorkshire, the launch provides another option combining boutique-style attention with broader market expertise. As the property market continues to shift, such strategic expansions may become more common amongst successful niche operators.

item.title: Renters’ Rights Act impact on agents drives website compliance reviews
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act in England has prompted letting agents to conduct thorough reviews of their online content to ensure compliance with the new legislation. As one of the most substantial reforms to rental laws in 30 years, the act requires agents to update all published information, including terms and conditions, legal statements, and consumer guidance across their digital platforms.

Hidden compliance risks in website content

Whilst many agencies have updated their main webpages, outdated information may still linger in less visible areas. Search engines often index older versions of pages or documents that remain on servers, even if they’re no longer linked from active site navigation. This creates potential compliance risks, as renters might encounter contradictory guidance when researching their rights.

Particular attention should be paid to PDF documents and archived pages, which may reference abolished tenancy concepts like Section 21 notices or fixed-term agreements. Property professionals might consider conducting comprehensive audits of their web presence, including:

  • Checking search engine results for obsolete pages
  • Reviewing all downloadable documents for accuracy
  • Implementing redirects for outdated content
  • Updating metadata to reflect current legislation

Industry response to legislative changes

Propertymark CEO Nathan Emerson acknowledged the sector’s efforts in preparing for the reforms, noting the “immense dedication and planning” involved in implementing the new framework. However, he emphasised the importance of ongoing checks to maintain compliance across all communication channels.

The legislation’s complexity means some agencies may need to revisit their digital content strategy. Outdated material could potentially mislead tenants about their rights or create confusion about landlords’ obligations. Letting agents might find it beneficial to work with web specialists to ensure their online presence fully reflects the current legal landscape.

As the rental sector adapts to these changes, maintaining accurate, consistent information across all platforms remains crucial for both compliance and customer trust. Regular content reviews could help agencies avoid potential issues whilst demonstrating their commitment to transparency under the new regime.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act in England has prompted letting agents to conduct thorough reviews of their online content to ensure compliance with the new legislation. As one of the most substantial reforms to rental laws in 30 years, the act requires agents to update all published information, including terms and conditions, legal statements, and consumer guidance across their digital platforms.

Hidden compliance risks in website content

Whilst many agencies have updated their main webpages, outdated information may still linger in less visible areas. Search engines often index older versions of pages or documents that remain on servers, even if they’re no longer linked from active site navigation. This creates potential compliance risks, as renters might encounter contradictory guidance when researching their rights.

Particular attention should be paid to PDF documents and archived pages, which may reference abolished tenancy concepts like Section 21 notices or fixed-term agreements. Property professionals might consider conducting comprehensive audits of their web presence, including:

  • Checking search engine results for obsolete pages
  • Reviewing all downloadable documents for accuracy
  • Implementing redirects for outdated content
  • Updating metadata to reflect current legislation

Industry response to legislative changes

Propertymark CEO Nathan Emerson acknowledged the sector’s efforts in preparing for the reforms, noting the “immense dedication and planning” involved in implementing the new framework. However, he emphasised the importance of ongoing checks to maintain compliance across all communication channels.

The legislation’s complexity means some agencies may need to revisit their digital content strategy. Outdated material could potentially mislead tenants about their rights or create confusion about landlords’ obligations. Letting agents might find it beneficial to work with web specialists to ensure their online presence fully reflects the current legal landscape.

As the rental sector adapts to these changes, maintaining accurate, consistent information across all platforms remains crucial for both compliance and customer trust. Regular content reviews could help agencies avoid potential issues whilst demonstrating their commitment to transparency under the new regime.

item.title: Is the landlord right to claim for a damaged Saniflo pump?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Macerator pumps, such as Saniflo systems, are a popular solution in properties where traditional plumbing is impractical. However, their increasing use has led to a rise in disputes between landlords and tenants over repair or replacement costs. A recent case highlights the importance of contractor reports and evidence in resolving such disagreements.

The case in question

A landlord sought to claim the cost of replacing a damaged Saniflo pump, arguing that the tenant’s misuse—specifically, flushing wipes—had caused the issue. The tenant had reported a toilet blockage, prompting an emergency call-out. The contractor inspected the pump and deemed it non-functional, recommending a replacement. However, their report did not mention tenant misuse or a blockage as the cause, simply stating that landlord authorisation was required for the repair.

When the case went to adjudication, the contractor’s report proved crucial. As it did not attribute the damage to tenant fault, the adjudicator ruled in the tenant’s favour, deciding they were not responsible for the replacement cost. This outcome underscores the need for landlords to ensure contractor reports are thorough and accurate when submitting claims.

The importance of deposit protection schemes

This case also highlights the role of deposit protection schemes, such as the Tenancy Deposit Scheme, in resolving disputes fairly. These schemes safeguard tenants’ deposits and rely on impartial adjudicators to make decisions based solely on the evidence provided. For landlords, ensuring claims are well-documented with clear, objective reports is essential to achieving a favourable outcome.

Tenants, meanwhile, may wish to report any issues promptly and in writing, as this can help demonstrate their adherence to their tenancy agreement. Both parties benefit from maintaining open communication and keeping detailed records of property maintenance and repairs.

Key takeaways for landlords and tenants

  • When a tenant reports an issue, the landlord must establish the cause. A contractor’s report can be pivotal in determining liability.
  • Landlords should ensure contractor reports are clear and detailed, as vague or incomplete documentation may weaken a claim.
  • Tenants should report problems promptly and follow usage guidelines for appliances like macerator pumps to avoid potential disputes.
  • Deposit protection schemes provide a fair and impartial process for resolving disagreements, benefiting both landlords and tenants.

This case serves as a reminder of the complexities surrounding macerator pump systems in rental properties. Both landlords and tenants may wish to prioritise clear communication and thorough documentation to minimise disputes and ensure fair outcomes.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Macerator pumps, such as Saniflo systems, are a popular solution in properties where traditional plumbing is impractical. However, their increasing use has led to a rise in disputes between landlords and tenants over repair or replacement costs. A recent case highlights the importance of contractor reports and evidence in resolving such disagreements.

The case in question

A landlord sought to claim the cost of replacing a damaged Saniflo pump, arguing that the tenant’s misuse—specifically, flushing wipes—had caused the issue. The tenant had reported a toilet blockage, prompting an emergency call-out. The contractor inspected the pump and deemed it non-functional, recommending a replacement. However, their report did not mention tenant misuse or a blockage as the cause, simply stating that landlord authorisation was required for the repair.

When the case went to adjudication, the contractor’s report proved crucial. As it did not attribute the damage to tenant fault, the adjudicator ruled in the tenant’s favour, deciding they were not responsible for the replacement cost. This outcome underscores the need for landlords to ensure contractor reports are thorough and accurate when submitting claims.

The importance of deposit protection schemes

This case also highlights the role of deposit protection schemes, such as the Tenancy Deposit Scheme, in resolving disputes fairly. These schemes safeguard tenants’ deposits and rely on impartial adjudicators to make decisions based solely on the evidence provided. For landlords, ensuring claims are well-documented with clear, objective reports is essential to achieving a favourable outcome.

Tenants, meanwhile, may wish to report any issues promptly and in writing, as this can help demonstrate their adherence to their tenancy agreement. Both parties benefit from maintaining open communication and keeping detailed records of property maintenance and repairs.

Key takeaways for landlords and tenants

  • When a tenant reports an issue, the landlord must establish the cause. A contractor’s report can be pivotal in determining liability.
  • Landlords should ensure contractor reports are clear and detailed, as vague or incomplete documentation may weaken a claim.
  • Tenants should report problems promptly and follow usage guidelines for appliances like macerator pumps to avoid potential disputes.
  • Deposit protection schemes provide a fair and impartial process for resolving disagreements, benefiting both landlords and tenants.

This case serves as a reminder of the complexities surrounding macerator pump systems in rental properties. Both landlords and tenants may wish to prioritise clear communication and thorough documentation to minimise disputes and ensure fair outcomes.

item.title: Market activity strengthens as demand recovers and supply remains strong
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

London Lettings Market Shows Signs of Recovery as Supply Holds Steady

The London lettings market displayed encouraging resilience in April, with improving demand and stable supply levels according to Foxtons' latest market review. As the final snapshot before the Renters’ Rights Act implementation, the data reveals a sector adjusting to new dynamics whilst maintaining balanced conditions. Supply remained comfortably above 2023 levels, with new listings up 3.7% annually, suggesting sustained landlord confidence despite regulatory changes.

Seasonal Shifts and Affordability Trends

Whilst April saw the expected 6% monthly dip in listings following March's seasonal peak, the market demonstrated robust fundamentals. Demand continued its gradual recovery with renter registrations rising 9.5% month-on-month, though remaining 6% below April 2023 levels. This suggests the market recovery, whilst underway, hasn't yet regained previous momentum.

Notably, affordability conditions remained stable with renters allocating 99% of budgets to housing costs - unchanged year-on-year. The marginal 0.1% monthly decline in spending indicates no material pressure on tenants' financial capacity. Competition between renters eased annually (down 6.5%) but increased 15.6% monthly, reflecting typical spring market activity.

Regional Variations and Operator Strategies

The data reveals striking regional disparities across London:

  • West London saw supply surge 40% annually alongside 17% demand growth
  • North London listings increased 34% despite 3% fewer registrations
  • Central London experienced 24% fewer new instructions and 16% reduced demand

Industry experts suggest operators succeeding in this competitive environment focus on core fundamentals: accurate pricing, transparent value propositions and streamlined rental processes. As the Build to Rent sector evolves, the emphasis appears to be striking the right balance between responsiveness and patience with market recovery.

The April figures paint a picture of a lettings market in transition - with supply comfortably meeting demand, stable affordability metrics, and gradual rather than dramatic recovery. As the sector adapts to new regulations, these balanced conditions may provide a stable foundation for the months ahead.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

London Lettings Market Shows Signs of Recovery as Supply Holds Steady

The London lettings market displayed encouraging resilience in April, with improving demand and stable supply levels according to Foxtons' latest market review. As the final snapshot before the Renters’ Rights Act implementation, the data reveals a sector adjusting to new dynamics whilst maintaining balanced conditions. Supply remained comfortably above 2023 levels, with new listings up 3.7% annually, suggesting sustained landlord confidence despite regulatory changes.

Seasonal Shifts and Affordability Trends

Whilst April saw the expected 6% monthly dip in listings following March's seasonal peak, the market demonstrated robust fundamentals. Demand continued its gradual recovery with renter registrations rising 9.5% month-on-month, though remaining 6% below April 2023 levels. This suggests the market recovery, whilst underway, hasn't yet regained previous momentum.

Notably, affordability conditions remained stable with renters allocating 99% of budgets to housing costs - unchanged year-on-year. The marginal 0.1% monthly decline in spending indicates no material pressure on tenants' financial capacity. Competition between renters eased annually (down 6.5%) but increased 15.6% monthly, reflecting typical spring market activity.

Regional Variations and Operator Strategies

The data reveals striking regional disparities across London:

  • West London saw supply surge 40% annually alongside 17% demand growth
  • North London listings increased 34% despite 3% fewer registrations
  • Central London experienced 24% fewer new instructions and 16% reduced demand

Industry experts suggest operators succeeding in this competitive environment focus on core fundamentals: accurate pricing, transparent value propositions and streamlined rental processes. As the Build to Rent sector evolves, the emphasis appears to be striking the right balance between responsiveness and patience with market recovery.

The April figures paint a picture of a lettings market in transition - with supply comfortably meeting demand, stable affordability metrics, and gradual rather than dramatic recovery. As the sector adapts to new regulations, these balanced conditions may provide a stable foundation for the months ahead.

item.title: Renters’ Rights Act changes for landlords risks leaving tenants with fewer options
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights growing concerns about how landlords are adapting to recent changes under the Renters’ Rights Act, with potential implications for tenant choice in England and Wales. The legislation, which represents the most significant shake-up of private rental sector rules in decades, appears to be creating unintended consequences as property owners grapple with its practical effects.

Landlord awareness gaps emerge

A survey of 650 landlords and tenants reveals significant knowledge gaps about key provisions in the Renters’ Rights Act. Fewer than one in three landlords fully understand that the law now prohibits advance rent payments exceeding one month. Whilst 43% recognise rules have changed, they remain unclear on specifics, and over a quarter were completely unaware of the payment restriction.

The legislation introduces several fundamental changes including the replacement of fixed-term tenancies with Assured Periodic Tenancies. These reforms aim to strengthen tenant protections, but the research suggests implementation challenges may be emerging. With many landlords still working through the practical implications, the sector appears to be in a transitional phase that could affect rental market dynamics.

Potential market impacts unfolding

The restrictions on advance rent payments appear to be influencing landlord behaviour in ways that might reduce housing options for some tenants. Nearly six in ten landlords expect to see more borderline or higher-risk applications, with only 10% anticipating improved applicant quality. This perception is leading to notable changes in how properties are let:

  • 38% of landlords are reconsidering their position or becoming more selective with tenants
  • 42% are increasingly relying on letting agents for applicant screening
  • 6% report becoming slightly more cautious about tenant selection

These shifts come alongside broader trends showing a steady increase in landlords planning to reduce their property portfolios. The combination of legislative changes and market pressures could potentially tighten supply in a sector where demand continues to outpace availability.

As the rental market adapts to these reforms, both landlords and tenants may need to navigate a period of adjustment. The full effects of the Renters’ Rights Act will likely become clearer as the sector gains more experience operating under the new framework.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New research highlights growing concerns about how landlords are adapting to recent changes under the Renters’ Rights Act, with potential implications for tenant choice in England and Wales. The legislation, which represents the most significant shake-up of private rental sector rules in decades, appears to be creating unintended consequences as property owners grapple with its practical effects.

Landlord awareness gaps emerge

A survey of 650 landlords and tenants reveals significant knowledge gaps about key provisions in the Renters’ Rights Act. Fewer than one in three landlords fully understand that the law now prohibits advance rent payments exceeding one month. Whilst 43% recognise rules have changed, they remain unclear on specifics, and over a quarter were completely unaware of the payment restriction.

The legislation introduces several fundamental changes including the replacement of fixed-term tenancies with Assured Periodic Tenancies. These reforms aim to strengthen tenant protections, but the research suggests implementation challenges may be emerging. With many landlords still working through the practical implications, the sector appears to be in a transitional phase that could affect rental market dynamics.

Potential market impacts unfolding

The restrictions on advance rent payments appear to be influencing landlord behaviour in ways that might reduce housing options for some tenants. Nearly six in ten landlords expect to see more borderline or higher-risk applications, with only 10% anticipating improved applicant quality. This perception is leading to notable changes in how properties are let:

  • 38% of landlords are reconsidering their position or becoming more selective with tenants
  • 42% are increasingly relying on letting agents for applicant screening
  • 6% report becoming slightly more cautious about tenant selection

These shifts come alongside broader trends showing a steady increase in landlords planning to reduce their property portfolios. The combination of legislative changes and market pressures could potentially tighten supply in a sector where demand continues to outpace availability.

As the rental market adapts to these reforms, both landlords and tenants may need to navigate a period of adjustment. The full effects of the Renters’ Rights Act will likely become clearer as the sector gains more experience operating under the new framework.

item.title: Furnishing Rental Bedrooms That Tenants Actually Want to Stay In
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords frequently prioritise kitchen upgrades and bathroom renovations when preparing rental properties, yet often overlook the importance of well-designed bedrooms. Given that tenants typically spend a third of their day in this private space, thoughtful bedroom furnishings could make a significant difference to tenant satisfaction and retention rates. Neglecting this key area might lead to shorter tenancies and increased void periods.

Why Bedroom Quality Matters

Research suggests that comfortable sleeping environments contribute to overall tenant contentment. A bedroom featuring worn-out mattresses, inadequate storage, or harsh lighting may prompt renters to consider alternative properties when their lease ends. Landlords might find that investing in quality bedroom furnishings could yield long-term benefits through:

  • Extended tenancy agreements
  • Reduced marketing and referencing costs between lets
  • Positive word-of-mouth recommendations

Key Considerations for Rental Bedrooms

Property owners looking to enhance their rental bedrooms might consider several practical elements:

  • Comfortable sleeping arrangements: A good-quality mattress with protective coverings often forms the foundation of tenant satisfaction
  • Functional storage solutions: Built-in wardrobes or under-bed storage can help maximise space in smaller rooms
  • Appropriate lighting: A combination of overhead lighting and bedside lamps accommodates different needs
  • Neutral decor: Light, neutral colours tend to appeal to a broader range of potential tenants

Whilst landlords need to balance investment costs with potential returns, creating inviting bedroom spaces could prove worthwhile. Simple touches like blackout curtains or USB charging points might enhance the rental proposition without substantial expenditure. Ultimately, properties that offer comfort and functionality throughout – including in the bedroom – may stand out in competitive rental markets.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords frequently prioritise kitchen upgrades and bathroom renovations when preparing rental properties, yet often overlook the importance of well-designed bedrooms. Given that tenants typically spend a third of their day in this private space, thoughtful bedroom furnishings could make a significant difference to tenant satisfaction and retention rates. Neglecting this key area might lead to shorter tenancies and increased void periods.

Why Bedroom Quality Matters

Research suggests that comfortable sleeping environments contribute to overall tenant contentment. A bedroom featuring worn-out mattresses, inadequate storage, or harsh lighting may prompt renters to consider alternative properties when their lease ends. Landlords might find that investing in quality bedroom furnishings could yield long-term benefits through:

  • Extended tenancy agreements
  • Reduced marketing and referencing costs between lets
  • Positive word-of-mouth recommendations

Key Considerations for Rental Bedrooms

Property owners looking to enhance their rental bedrooms might consider several practical elements:

  • Comfortable sleeping arrangements: A good-quality mattress with protective coverings often forms the foundation of tenant satisfaction
  • Functional storage solutions: Built-in wardrobes or under-bed storage can help maximise space in smaller rooms
  • Appropriate lighting: A combination of overhead lighting and bedside lamps accommodates different needs
  • Neutral decor: Light, neutral colours tend to appeal to a broader range of potential tenants

Whilst landlords need to balance investment costs with potential returns, creating inviting bedroom spaces could prove worthwhile. Simple touches like blackout curtains or USB charging points might enhance the rental proposition without substantial expenditure. Ultimately, properties that offer comfort and functionality throughout – including in the bedroom – may stand out in competitive rental markets.

item.title: Central London’s Private Office Market: Best Locations and Operators for Businesses to Know
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Central London's office market is witnessing a notable shift towards high-quality workspaces, with Grade A properties maintaining strong demand whilst secondary office stock faces declining interest. Industry reports from leading analysts suggest this "flight to quality" trend has defined the capital's commercial property landscape throughout 2024 and shows no signs of abating.

Why Premium Workspaces Remain in Demand

Businesses seeking Central London offices appear increasingly focused on modern, flexible spaces that support hybrid working models and sustainability goals. Grade A properties typically offer superior amenities, energy efficiency credentials, and adaptable floorplans – features that have become non-negotiable for many firms post-pandemic. Market data indicates that whilst overall office occupancy rates fluctuate, premium spaces in prime locations continue to command stable rental values.

Key Central London Districts for Quality Offices

Several Central London neighbourhoods stand out for businesses prioritising quality workspace:

  • City of London – The traditional financial hub maintains its appeal, particularly for firms requiring proximity to banking institutions and transport links
  • Midtown – The area between the City and West End offers a balance of prestige and relative value, attracting professional services firms
  • Paddington – Benefiting from Crossrail connections, this emerging business district provides modern developments at competitive rates

Market analysts note that businesses might consider these areas when evaluating office relocation or expansion plans, as each offers distinct advantages depending on company requirements and budget considerations.

The Central London office market's bifurcation between prime and secondary stock presents both challenges and opportunities. Businesses reviewing their property strategy may find current conditions favourable for securing high-quality workspace, particularly in well-connected locations meeting modern workforce expectations.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Central London's office market is witnessing a notable shift towards high-quality workspaces, with Grade A properties maintaining strong demand whilst secondary office stock faces declining interest. Industry reports from leading analysts suggest this "flight to quality" trend has defined the capital's commercial property landscape throughout 2024 and shows no signs of abating.

Why Premium Workspaces Remain in Demand

Businesses seeking Central London offices appear increasingly focused on modern, flexible spaces that support hybrid working models and sustainability goals. Grade A properties typically offer superior amenities, energy efficiency credentials, and adaptable floorplans – features that have become non-negotiable for many firms post-pandemic. Market data indicates that whilst overall office occupancy rates fluctuate, premium spaces in prime locations continue to command stable rental values.

Key Central London Districts for Quality Offices

Several Central London neighbourhoods stand out for businesses prioritising quality workspace:

  • City of London – The traditional financial hub maintains its appeal, particularly for firms requiring proximity to banking institutions and transport links
  • Midtown – The area between the City and West End offers a balance of prestige and relative value, attracting professional services firms
  • Paddington – Benefiting from Crossrail connections, this emerging business district provides modern developments at competitive rates

Market analysts note that businesses might consider these areas when evaluating office relocation or expansion plans, as each offers distinct advantages depending on company requirements and budget considerations.

The Central London office market's bifurcation between prime and secondary stock presents both challenges and opportunities. Businesses reviewing their property strategy may find current conditions favourable for securing high-quality workspace, particularly in well-connected locations meeting modern workforce expectations.

item.title: ‘Is Manchester a Good Place to Buy or Invest in Property?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Manchester has cemented its position as one of the UK’s most compelling property markets, attracting both homebuyers and investors with its competitive pricing and strong rental demand. Whilst London traditionally dominates headlines, the Northern powerhouse offers distinct advantages that may appeal to those seeking value and growth potential.

Why Manchester Stands Out for Property Investment

Several factors contribute to Manchester’s appeal for property purchases. The city consistently delivers higher rental yields than many southern counterparts, with average returns often exceeding 5% in popular postcodes. This outperforms London’s typical yields whilst requiring lower initial capital outlay.

The regional economy continues to strengthen, with major employers like the BBC and numerous tech firms establishing northern hubs. This employment growth fuels housing demand from young professionals, particularly in areas such as Salford Quays and the city centre. Infrastructure projects like HS2, though delayed, promise to enhance connectivity further.

Market Dynamics Buyers Should Consider

Manchester’s property prices have shown resilient growth, increasing by approximately 40% over the past decade according to Land Registry data. However, average values remain significantly below London’s, with entry points around £200,000 for city centre flats. This relative affordability makes the market accessible to first-time buyers and portfolio builders alike.

Prospective investors might note:

  • Strong tenant demand from students and young professionals
  • Ongoing regeneration across districts like Ancoats and New Islington
  • Council support for build-to-rent developments
  • Comparatively low void periods versus national averages

That said, buyers should research specific neighbourhoods thoroughly, as performance varies considerably between postcodes. Areas with new transport links or university expansions often show particularly strong fundamentals.

Manchester’s property market presents an intriguing alternative to pricier southern cities, combining robust yields with genuine growth potential. As always, thorough due diligence remains essential before making any purchase decision.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Manchester has cemented its position as one of the UK’s most compelling property markets, attracting both homebuyers and investors with its competitive pricing and strong rental demand. Whilst London traditionally dominates headlines, the Northern powerhouse offers distinct advantages that may appeal to those seeking value and growth potential.

Why Manchester Stands Out for Property Investment

Several factors contribute to Manchester’s appeal for property purchases. The city consistently delivers higher rental yields than many southern counterparts, with average returns often exceeding 5% in popular postcodes. This outperforms London’s typical yields whilst requiring lower initial capital outlay.

The regional economy continues to strengthen, with major employers like the BBC and numerous tech firms establishing northern hubs. This employment growth fuels housing demand from young professionals, particularly in areas such as Salford Quays and the city centre. Infrastructure projects like HS2, though delayed, promise to enhance connectivity further.

Market Dynamics Buyers Should Consider

Manchester’s property prices have shown resilient growth, increasing by approximately 40% over the past decade according to Land Registry data. However, average values remain significantly below London’s, with entry points around £200,000 for city centre flats. This relative affordability makes the market accessible to first-time buyers and portfolio builders alike.

Prospective investors might note:

  • Strong tenant demand from students and young professionals
  • Ongoing regeneration across districts like Ancoats and New Islington
  • Council support for build-to-rent developments
  • Comparatively low void periods versus national averages

That said, buyers should research specific neighbourhoods thoroughly, as performance varies considerably between postcodes. Areas with new transport links or university expansions often show particularly strong fundamentals.

Manchester’s property market presents an intriguing alternative to pricier southern cities, combining robust yields with genuine growth potential. As always, thorough due diligence remains essential before making any purchase decision.

item.title: Six ways the Renters Rights Act will change renting: Part 7, Looking ahead
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Renters' Reform Act has now been in force for several weeks, yet many landlords, tenants and letting agents remain unaware of the full implications of these sweeping changes to the private rental sector. As the dust settles on this landmark legislation, it's becoming clearer how these reforms may reshape the rental landscape in England.

Key objectives of the reforms

The government's ambitious reforms aim to deliver several fundamental improvements to the private rental market:

  • Greater security for tenants through the abolition of Section 21 'no fault' evictions
  • More predictable rent increases with clearer notice periods
  • Fairer tenant selection processes, banning blanket bans on benefit claimants or families with children
  • Stronger protections against rogue landlords through enhanced enforcement powers
  • Improved routes for tenants to challenge poor conditions through a new ombudsman scheme

Implementation challenges ahead

While the intentions behind the Act are broadly welcomed, significant challenges remain in translating these reforms into tangible improvements for renters:

  • Local authorities will need substantial resources to properly enforce against criminal landlords
  • The courts system requires modernisation to handle expected increases in possession cases
  • Rent controls remain absent from the legislation, leaving affordability concerns unaddressed
  • The private rental sector continues to face chronic undersupply in many areas

As these reforms bed in over the coming months, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the new rules. While the Act represents the most significant shake-up of renting in a generation, its ultimate success will depend on effective implementation and adequate resourcing for enforcement bodies.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Renters' Reform Act has now been in force for several weeks, yet many landlords, tenants and letting agents remain unaware of the full implications of these sweeping changes to the private rental sector. As the dust settles on this landmark legislation, it's becoming clearer how these reforms may reshape the rental landscape in England.

Key objectives of the reforms

The government's ambitious reforms aim to deliver several fundamental improvements to the private rental market:

  • Greater security for tenants through the abolition of Section 21 'no fault' evictions
  • More predictable rent increases with clearer notice periods
  • Fairer tenant selection processes, banning blanket bans on benefit claimants or families with children
  • Stronger protections against rogue landlords through enhanced enforcement powers
  • Improved routes for tenants to challenge poor conditions through a new ombudsman scheme

Implementation challenges ahead

While the intentions behind the Act are broadly welcomed, significant challenges remain in translating these reforms into tangible improvements for renters:

  • Local authorities will need substantial resources to properly enforce against criminal landlords
  • The courts system requires modernisation to handle expected increases in possession cases
  • Rent controls remain absent from the legislation, leaving affordability concerns unaddressed
  • The private rental sector continues to face chronic undersupply in many areas

As these reforms bed in over the coming months, both landlords and tenants may wish to familiarise themselves with the new rules. While the Act represents the most significant shake-up of renting in a generation, its ultimate success will depend on effective implementation and adequate resourcing for enforcement bodies.

item.title: Tributes paid following death of property industry stalwart
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property industry is mourning the loss of Clive Emson MBE, the charismatic auctioneer and television personality who passed away aged 79. The founder of Clive Emson Land and Property Auctioneers died peacefully at home in Kent on Wednesday, leaving behind a significant legacy in the property sector and beyond.

A property auction pioneer

Emson built one of Britain's most recognisable regional auction businesses from humble beginnings, expanding his operation across southern England with offices in Kent, Essex, Hampshire and Devon. His catchphrase "have gavel, will travel" became synonymous with his hands-on approach to property auctions.

Many homeowners and investors will remember Emson from his regular appearances on BBC's Homes Under the Hammer, where his expertise and affable personality made him a favourite among viewers. His company became known for making property auctions accessible to both seasoned investors and first-time buyers alike.

Beyond the property world

Emson's contributions extended far beyond the auction room. He was awarded an MBE in recognition of his charitable work supporting vulnerable young people in Kent. Colleagues described him as a generous mentor who prioritised helping others throughout his career.

Sam Kinloch, a director at Clive Emson Auctioneers, paid tribute to his former boss: "His integrity, humour and passion for life shone through in everything he did. He had this remarkable ability to make everyone feel valued, whether they were colleagues, clients or complete strangers."

Emson is survived by his two children, who continue to work in the family business he established. The property community has lost not just a talented professional, but a true character whose enthusiasm for auctions and helping others left a lasting impression on all who knew him.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property industry is mourning the loss of Clive Emson MBE, the charismatic auctioneer and television personality who passed away aged 79. The founder of Clive Emson Land and Property Auctioneers died peacefully at home in Kent on Wednesday, leaving behind a significant legacy in the property sector and beyond.

A property auction pioneer

Emson built one of Britain's most recognisable regional auction businesses from humble beginnings, expanding his operation across southern England with offices in Kent, Essex, Hampshire and Devon. His catchphrase "have gavel, will travel" became synonymous with his hands-on approach to property auctions.

Many homeowners and investors will remember Emson from his regular appearances on BBC's Homes Under the Hammer, where his expertise and affable personality made him a favourite among viewers. His company became known for making property auctions accessible to both seasoned investors and first-time buyers alike.

Beyond the property world

Emson's contributions extended far beyond the auction room. He was awarded an MBE in recognition of his charitable work supporting vulnerable young people in Kent. Colleagues described him as a generous mentor who prioritised helping others throughout his career.

Sam Kinloch, a director at Clive Emson Auctioneers, paid tribute to his former boss: "His integrity, humour and passion for life shone through in everything he did. He had this remarkable ability to make everyone feel valued, whether they were colleagues, clients or complete strangers."

Emson is survived by his two children, who continue to work in the family business he established. The property community has lost not just a talented professional, but a true character whose enthusiasm for auctions and helping others left a lasting impression on all who knew him.
